item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the use of these candies are very interesting. I love the addition of Dr. Wondertainment, as it represents one of my favorite GoIs. Dr. Wondertainment SCPs and anomalies are one of my favorites!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done. The note by Wondertainment at the end pushed me from novote to +1 territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe these were made for a different species? Some of Wondertainments' products seems like they're made for different species.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a big problem with the fatalities, as they don't really fit with Wondertainment. One thing that is usually consistent with Wondertainments products that, unless you expressly ignore the safety warnings given, they won't kill you. It's hard to expect children to follow those safety warnings, which is what gives the Wondertainment products a creepy undertone, but they aren't immediately lethal. Consumer products that WILL kill the users are more of the Factory's claim to fame.

I don't mind the "jumping out to of orbit," as the safety notice warned to not use it out doors. However, the VENUSIAN BERRY, JOVIAN JELLY, and PLANET X all seem like they are highly lethal even if you follow the instructions.

I would personally add a note that the class D's were taking more than the recommended "one per every four hours" limit if you do want to just kill them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1916-6 is scary, yo
+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor thing: the picture breaks disbelief a bit as it is clearly made out of glass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have qualms about this one. Stylistically, I feel like Wondertainment would have made candies for all of the planets. However, from a reader's view, the planets that weren't covered probably wouldn't have done much for the article (although I would have loved to see a Saturn candy that could make the consumer float).

Still a +1, though. I imagine Planet X as turning the subject into a miniature black hole.

Edit: If the D-Class had trackers implanted subdermally, shouldn't we know where the moon D-Class is? Unless the tracker operated with GPS, it should still be transmitting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the moment at which I upvoted, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think they believe everyone casually owns spacesuits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That redacted part seems rather…lazy.
I'd either put something there, or put "as well as significant quantities of an unknown [REDACTED]"
Something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really interesting and original. Don't see SCPs like this very often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good, but I agree with a previous comment I have seen here: The candy seems almost TOO lethal for your usual "Uncle doing crazy stuff with kids parents wouldnt do at all" Dr. Wondertainment stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, but there's one glaring detail that's irritating me more as I read comments arguing it: kilograms are not units of force/weight. They never will be units of force/weight. If you want to use kilograms instead of newtons or pounds or something, then it needs to be specified that subjects' weights are altered to the equivalent of X kilograms under standard gravitational acceleration, as is suggested earlier on in the comments.

Again, I do like this entry overall, and I see it's doing well as is, but I personally believe opportunities for improvement should never be passed up, regardless of their significance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw these and couldn't help but be reminded of this entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article made me feel sick, and the whole gravity affecting aspect annoyed me, but overall the article was well written and presented. +1

EDIT: Changed overwhole to overall, because I'm OCD about repeating my words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't necessarily make assumptions like that when dealing with anomalous objects. (Though I do agree with the conclusion for other reasons — density is defined in terms of mass and volume, so changing mass without changing volume actually does have to change the density.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm fairly ambivalent about the article, but the picture bugs me. That's not candy, it's a glass ornament crafted in the shape of candy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this in general, but two of the tests seem stupid to the point of lolfoundation.

Moon candy: We know these things affect mass, we know the subject's mass has been reduced without a compensating reduction of leg strength - why on earth did we think it was a good idea to test outside? "Because it's funny" is a legitimate answer, but not for a mainlist SCP.

Planet X Candy: We know absolutely nothing about what the possible effects of this thing might be, and yet we test it in a location where the effect is capable of dangerously irradiating hundreds of Foundation staff? This is horrific, but it's horror caused by gross negligence. I don't buy it.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only at the SCP Foundation would a discussion of magic candy lead to a scholarly argument about the mathematics of gravity and atmospheric drag. I love you guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At low velocities, only viscous drag matters as opposed to form drag, and reynolds number for air is big,hence noep.

Noone is saying you'd achieve moon's escape velocity, but for the zero gravity case , I don't think air drag matters enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm dumb, I forgot to factor in the largest factor in that case - the centrifugal force, at 0.003 g in the direction of the celestial orb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good job remember the Sizzler though. But yes, she turned into a blueberry. It wasn't technically weight gain, but that being the effect of this is all I can think of now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Drag goes as a complicated function of velocity, it's modeled as v at low velocities, v squared at higher, presumably it goes above that. I can't be doing with crunching the numbers but I think drag would be important and for the moon you wouldn't escape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the description for -4, realized it was Jupiter, and I gleed.
Then I saw it was a Wondertainment product, and I gleed more.
… and then I saw what it actually does, and had to be physically restrained from violating the +1 button. o.o

One little nitpick though, stylistically… I don't think you need to (or should) mention specifically that the patterns on -3 look like Earth's Moon. It feels like it's giving the game away just a bit early. If you still want to keep some descriptor regarding the pattern of the white and black spots, you could say something more like, "D-class personnel have likened the pattern to a rough image of a human face", but flat-out saying that the candy looks like the Moon feels spoiler-y.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was there a Pluto-flavored candy, or did they stop making them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wrapper in the image looks strange to me, like it's a molded piece of solid sugar that's been made to look like a wrapper.

Prominent signage is to be placed on and inside the crate indicating that SCP-1916 is non-edible.

I would think that a warning like that wouldn't be required for people working for the Foundation. I mean, if they had so little common sense that they'd eat something found in a Foundation warehouse, would they have gotten a job with Foundation that allowed them access to that warehouse?

[DATA EXPUNGED]. 82 fatalities at test facility due to exposure to gamma radiation; 438 non-lethal instances of radiation poisoning. Remains of subject have not been located.

Did the gravity become so extreme that the atoms in the subject's body underwent fusion? If so, wouldn't enough fusion to cause fatal radiation poisoning also cause a huge explosion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the change of mass requires the addition or subtraction of matter from the object, gaining or losing mass would make the subject bigger or smaller, or alter their density. If the candies affect the gravitational force exerted upon the subject, determining a change of mass might be as easy as measuring their own gravity with the use of a very sensitive accelerometer, or a torsion balance, testing against a known mass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your definition of "high-jinks" may vary from that used by Dr. Wondertainment

Upvoted +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could swear I read somewhere once that a running leap on the moon would be sufficient enough that you wouldn't come back down. (Or maybe it was driving a golf ball.)

In any event, I was sort of working from the idea that these candies are based on a 1950s-ish "SCIENCE!" interpretation of how other planets worked - thus the references on the ad copy to Mars having canals, the Great Red Spot being an ocean, etc. The different rates of gravity experienced by the user are correct, but Wondertainment took some liberty with the other effects for high-jink related purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been awhile since i've seen the movies - didn't Violet get turned into a giant blueberry? I wasn't going for any intentional similarity there - I just wanted a candy-related reference for the MTF name, and remembered the creepy old guy who played Wonka's rival in the '70s version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chalk it up to poor quality testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Veeery nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one. Each candy had a nice, unique effect and the test log was neat. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno.

I liked the Willy Wonka reference in the MTF name, but then I read the effects and I feel like it's too close to what happened to Violet, at least at the outset. That, and why is the jawbreaker in the picture wrapped in glass? I mean, it says "wrapper", but that's obviously glass at the ends.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As for the zero gravity one…. I'm not too sure. If I recall right, aerodynamic drag goes up with the cube of velocity - given this person wasn't going all that quickly, the drag effect would likely be negligible. It's a sort of situation that can't quite happen normally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kilograms can be units of mass, or weight/force.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You would measure how quickly they accelerated when a given force was applied (Newton's law: F = ma). For example, put them on roller skates or a skateboard, pull them along using a force measuring device (a simple hanging scale, pulled horizontally, would do), make a video recording, and examine it frame by frame to measure acceleration. (Any Mythbusters fan will be familiar with this technique :) )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that the poor sucker who tried the "Planet X" one somehow turned into a nuclear warhead. That being said, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of these are a little too malicious for Wondertainment. I can't see them making something that would kill you just from use and nothing else, like the Venus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gravity is measured by acceleration, e.g. earth standard is 9.81 m/s2. Mass is in kilograms1 and weight is in Newtons. I have a mass of ~70kg and rounding off acceleration due to gravity to 10 m/s2, I have a weight of 700 N. Kilograms are commonly used as a measure of weight in lay speak due to the fact that gravity is held constant on the surface of the earth, a scientific document studying variation in gravity would use Newtons. Speaking of which, there are tests they could have done to find if it was indeed a change in mass or gravitational acceleration that led to a change in weight.

Footnotes
1. The modern equivalent unit in the British Engineering System is pounds mass, however many people learned the now depreciated slugs as the unit of mass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One issue - the moon's escape velocity is 2.3 kilometers per second. A jump isn't going to do it. Even if they were under effect of the zero-weight candy, air friction would eventually stop them (unless the candy affects that too)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you… alright, I'll just put this aside for now. On a related note, how exactly do you imagine one would go about determining that any of the subject's mass was the same if their weight changed? You know, being that mass is calculated indirectly from weight and g.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eating the candy affects the gravitational force exerted on the subject, so I believe it would be correct to say it affects their weight, while their mass remains the same. Since "weight" and "mass" are generally synonymous on Earth, and since we don't normally refer to a person's weight in Newtons, it seems to me that convention would dictate that one describe the weight difference in terms of kilograms, and assume that "subject's weight is reduced to X kilograms" is synonymous with "gravitational force exerted on the subject is reduced to an amount comparable to X kilograms in standard Earth gravity".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is neat.

I hate you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last time I checked, weight was a measurement of the force gravity exerts on an object, and mass is just mass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't weight defined as "the force exerted by an object due to gravity" or something like that?

It's just, after learning the correct terms, the 'norm' being incorrect is irritating. I'm not even sure if the Imperial system even has a unit for mass as opposed to weight, which… has nothing to do with metric.

Even if an object would weigh less or more at non-Earth gravity, it would have the same amount of mass regardless of the gravitational force.

This is just a minor nitpick though; if more people like it the way it currently is, then the status quo is probably preferred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically, the Newton is the unit for force, and i've never heard it used in the context of "On the Moon you would only weigh X Newtons".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this and find it interesting, but…

Kilograms are the SI/Metric unit of mass, not of weight. I believe that Newtons (N) are the actual SI/Metric unit for weight.
